file_name: Parekh et al. - 2024 - The depth estimation and visualization of dermatological lesions Development and usability study.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The study used a convolutional neural network (CNN) for classifying skin lesions as benign or malignant (achieving 86% accuracy) and employed explainable artificial intelligence (XAI) techniques, specifically gradient class activation map (GradCAM), to localize lesion features. These AI methods were integral to the pipeline for depth estimation and visualization of dermatological lesions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled as an 'Original Paper' and a 'Development and Usability Study'. It presents original experimental work involving the development of a novel methodology for depth estimation using CNNs, XAI (GradCAM), and computer graphics, along with reported results (86% accuracy) and dermatologist feedback, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: The Depth Estimation and Visualization of Dermatological Lesions: Development and Usability Study
brief_summary: This study developed a novel methodology for the noninvasive depth estimation and 3D visualization of dermatological lesions to aid in melanoma staging. The pipeline utilized a convolutional neural network for benign versus malignant classification and GradCAM for feature localization, followed by computer graphics techniques like depth-from-defocus and Gabor filters to generate conical holograms. The model was trained and evaluated on the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) dataset, achieving an 86% accuracy in lesion classification. Qualitative analysis revealed that malignant lesions exhibited deeper conical sections and higher concentrations of 'red spots' compared to benign cases. A collaborating dermatologist provided positive feedback on the tool's potential for clinical implementation, suggesting further evaluation on diverse datasets like HAM10000.

lesion_types_studied.details: Used as one of the two binary classification labels in the CNN model trained on the ISIC dataset (1800 benign images).

Used as one of the two binary classification labels in the CNN model trained on the ISIC dataset (1497 malignant images). The paper notes that malignant lesions in this dataset primarily included melanoma and nevus.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions as benign or malignant
all_task_types.details: The study used a convolutional neural network (CNN) to classify dermatological lesions into two discrete diagnostic categories: benign and malignant. The model achieved an accuracy of 86% on the ISIC dataset. While the paper also performs depth estimation and 3D visualization, the primary AI prediction task reported with standard classification metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, Matthews correlation coefficient) is the binary classification of lesion malignancy.

datasets_used.name: International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dataset of processed skin cancer images used for training and evaluating the CNN model. The dataset was described as well-balanced with specific counts for benign and malignant classes.

use.training: Used to train the convolutional neural network (CNN) for binary classification of lesions as benign or malignant.
use.validation: Used for validation during the model training process.
use.testing: Used for testing the model's classification accuracy and evaluating the pipeline output.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000 (Human Against Machine with 10000 Dermoscopic Images)
datasets_used.description: A public dataset mentioned by the collaborating dermatologist as a suggestion for future evaluation. It includes complex classification requirements with classes like basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, and Merkel cell carcinoma.

use.other: Suggested for future evaluation and generalization of the methodology.

metrics_used_to_evaluate_results: Area under the receiver operating characteristics curve (AUC-ROC)
Confusion matrix values (True Positive, False Positive, True Negative, False Negative)

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To calculate precision, recall, F1-score, and Matthews correlation coefficient by providing true positive, false positive, true negative, and false negative values.
To evaluate binary classification performance, providing a more informative score than accuracy or F1-score by considering all four categories of the confusion matrix.
To represent the true positive–false positive trade-off and calculate the Area Under the Curve (AUC-ROC).
To represent the precision-recall trade-off and calculate the Area Under the Curve (AUC-PR), particularly important for mildly imbalanced datasets.

statistical_methods_used.method: Qualitative observation of red spot distribution
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To compare the depth and concentration of infection (red spots) between benign and malignant lesions across different depth thresholds in the 3D conical representation.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It relied on classification metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, MCC, AUC-ROC, AUC-PR) and qualitative observations of 3D hologram outputs (e.g., concentration of red spots, depth of conical sections) to compare benign and malignant lesions.

ISIC_dataset.experiment: Red spot analysis and 3D conical representation

Red Spot Analysis.benign_cases_observation: Lighter-colored lesions not heavily distinguished; lack of significant defocus boundary; conic surface heavily represented between 0 and -2 z values; fewer red spots at deeper layers.

models.Malignant_cases_observation: Better distinguished by GradCAM; defocus map takes specific boundary-like structure; significantly greater number of colored spots in 3D representation; conic structure had significant concentration after -2 z value, extending to greater depth; malignant tumors deeper, extending to 'Below 900' depth units in some cases; higher volume of spots beneath skin depth of 100 units and onwards.

ISIC_dataset.notes: Qualitative results from the testing pipeline showed that malignant lesions had a higher concentration of red spots (infection) in the upper and deeper portions of the skin compared to benign cases. Malignant cases exhibited deeper conical sections. One malignant case (Figure 3D) had an anomalous black outline which was discarded to avoid faulty output.

base_model_configuration.layers: Convolution blocks 1 and 2 had two convolution layers; blocks 3, 4, and 5 consisted of four. Each block included one pooling layer.

layers: Flatten layer applied to base model output
5 dense layers with ReLU activation function
Final dense layer of one unit with sigmoid activation function

classification_or_output_head.output: Probability of malignancy (binary classification: Benign vs Malignant)

preprocessing_techniques: Image resizing to uniform distribution of shape (224, 224, 3)
Label encoding of 'Benign' and 'Malignant' annotations using LabelEncoder from sklearn
Canny Edge detection for extracting edges and boundaries
Gaussian blur applied to the entire image for smoothing and noise reduction
Minimum-maximum normalization applied to the defocus map
conclusions: The proposed methodology successfully maps CNN classification outputs (benign vs. malignant) to 3D holographic representations of lesion depth.

Qualitative analysis of the 3D holograms reveals that malignant lesions exhibit a higher concentration of 'red spots' (indicating infection) in both the upper and deeper portions of the skin compared to benign cases.

Malignant cases demonstrate deeper conical sections in the 3D visualization, extending to greater depths (beyond -2 z values) than benign cases, which are primarily represented between 0 and -2 z values.
These qualitative depth and infection patterns observed in the holograms align with the initial binary classification performed by the neural model, validating the pipeline's consistency.

The CNN model achieved an accuracy of 86%, with a Matthews correlation coefficient of 0.71, precision of 0.84, recall of 0.86, and F1-score of 0.85, indicating satisfactory performance for benign versus malignant classification.

A collaborating dermatologist provided positive feedback, considering the tool potentially useful for clinical implementation and depth estimation, and suggested further evaluation on diverse datasets like HAM10000 before clinical introduction.

The study acknowledges limitations regarding the need for thorough evaluation on diverse datasets and handling anomalous image features (such as black outlines) that could produce faulty depth outputs.
important_remarks: The study lacks external validation on diverse datasets; the authors explicitly note that the ISIC dataset is skewed toward lighter skin tones, potentially impacting applicability to darker skin tones.
The methodology has not yet been compared against biopsy reports, which are considered the gold standard for imaging diagnostics and lesion depth assessment.
The impact of the GradCAM explainable AI component on the model's classification performance has not been quantified or isolated.
The qualitative depth estimation relies on 'red spot analysis' and 3D conical representations, which are novel and require further clinical evaluation before implementation.
The pipeline is sensitive to anomalous image features, such as black outlines, which can produce faulty depth outputs and were discarded in the analysis.
Clinical relevance is currently supported only by positive feedback from a single collaborating dermatologist rather than rigorous clinical trials or multi-center studies.
